faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.whatAreCreditsAnswer: Credits are consumed when your agents use AI models. Different models consume credits at different rates. For example, a simple task might use a few credits, while a complex multi-step workflow uses more.
faq.changePlans: Can I change plans at any time?
faq.changePlansAnswer: Yes! You can upgrade or downgrade your plan at any time. Changes take effect immediately, and we'll prorate charges accordingly.
faq.runOutOfCredits: What happens when I run out of credits?
faq.runOutOfCreditsAnswer: When your credits are depleted, your agents will stop running. You can purchase additional credits via auto-recharge, or upgrade to a higher plan for more monthly credits.
faq.doCreditsRollOverAnswer: On the Free plan, starter credits don't expire but don't replenish. On Pro, unused credits roll over for 1 month. On Team, credits roll over for 1 month.
faq.bringOwnModel: Can I bring my own model provider?
faq.bringOwnModelAnswer: Yes! All plans support bringing your own LLM API keys (Anthropic, etc.). When using your own keys, no VM0 credits are consumed for model usage.
faq.howSecureAnswer: Every agent run executes in an isolated Firecracker microVM with hardware-level KVM isolation. Credentials are injected at the network layer and never exposed to agent code. All agent HTTP/HTTPS traffic is logged with SHA-256 integrity per run.
faq.annualBilling: Do you offer annual billing or discounts?
faq.annualBillingAnswer: Contact us to discuss volume pricing, annual billing discounts, and custom arrangements for your team.

sentry-triage.title: Turn Sentry noise into a prioritized action list
sentry-triage.description: Ask Zero to pull your top unresolved Sentry errors, ranked by frequency. Zero reads the stack traces, explains the root cause in plain language, and tells you which file to fix first.

headings.scenario: Why Sentry triage wastes engineering time
headings.prompt: How to ask Zero to triage Sentry errors
headings.steps: How Zero analyzes and ranks your Sentry errors
headings.nextActions: Turn Sentry triage into GitHub issues and daily automation
headings.tips: Best practices for automated Sentry triage

sentry-triage.scenario: Monday morning. You open Slack and there are a dozen Sentry alerts in #dev. Most of them are noise, flaky tests, known issues, transient errors that resolve themselves. But somewhere in there might be something real: a broken integration, a billing bug, a crash that's hitting actual users. You don't want to open Sentry's dashboard and click through issues one by one. You just want to know: which ones matter, how bad are they, and what's causing them.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt:
@Zero take a look at the recent failures on Sentry. Please rank the failures from the last 24 hours by their occurrence frequency.

For the top three failures, provide me with a report that includes the expanded, detailed error stacks.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero top 3 Sentry errors in the last 24h, ranked by count.

promptVariants.label: Scheduled
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero every weekday at 9am, run this same Sentry check and post results to #dev. Only include errors with 5+ occurrences.

slackPreview.name: Yuma
slackPreview.text: @Zero look at recent failures on Sentry. Rank by frequency. Top 3 with detailed error stacks.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Here's the full Sentry failure report.

#	Issue	Count	Priority
1	Slack missing_scope	57	High
2	Unknown Stripe price ID	15	High
3	ZeroSidebar .map()	10	Med

steps.title: Zero connects to Sentry
steps.description: Zero pulls unresolved issues from your Sentry project for the specified time window and ranks them by occurrence count.

steps.title: Summary table
steps.description: Zero replies in the same Slack thread with a quick summary table, issue name, occurrence count, and priority level, that you can scan in 5 seconds.

steps.title: Detailed analysis
steps.description: For each top issue, Zero provides annotated stack traces and plain-language root cause explanations. For example, it doesn't just say "Unknown Stripe price ID", it explains that a new price was likely created in Stripe but the corresponding mapping was not updated.

nextActions.title: Turn findings into action
nextActions.description: Create GitHub issues from the report
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero create GitHub issues for #1 and #2. Assign #1 to Lancy (Slack scope fix) and #2 to James (Stripe mapping). Label both as bug, priority high.

nextActions.title: Go deeper
nextActions.description: Ask Zero to investigate a specific error
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero for issue #1, what exact Slack OAuth scope is missing? Check our app manifest and tell me what to add.

nextActions.title: Make it a routine
nextActions.description: Schedule this as a daily check
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero every weekday at 9am, run this same Sentry check and post results to #dev. Only include errors with 5+ occurrences.

integrations.description: OAuth connection to your Sentry org. Zero needs read access to issues and events.
Only needed if you want Zero to create issues from the report. Read/write access to issues.

tips: Be specific about the time window. "last 24 hours" for daily triage, "errors since the last deploy" for post-deploy checks.
Add a severity filter to reduce noise, only show errors with 10+ occurrences or skip errors tagged as known-issue.
Chain it into your standup, combine with the standup use case for a morning brief that includes both your task summary and the Sentry report.

standup-summary.title: Get your standup ready without opening 5 apps
standup-summary.description: Zero pulls from calendar, email, and Linear, then writes a shareable summary you can paste straight into the team thread.

headings.scenario: The daily standup scramble across Calendar, Gmail, and Linear
headings.prompt: How to ask Zero for a standup summary
headings.steps: How Zero compiles your work summary from 3 tools
headings.nextActions: Post to Slack, add blockers, or automate daily
headings.integrations: Required integrations: Google Calendar, Gmail, and Linear
headings.tips: Best practices for AI-generated standup summaries

standup-summary.scenario: It's 9:50 AM and standup starts in 10 minutes. You haven't checked your calendar yet. You ask Zero to pull yesterday's meetings, emails, and Linear tasks, it writes a summary you can paste straight into the team thread.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero check my calendar, emails, and Linear tasks since yesterday and write me a work summary. Include meeting highlights and PR status.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero what did I do yesterday? Check calendar and Linear.

promptVariants.label: Scheduled
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero every weekday at 9:45am, write my standup summary and DM it to me.

slackPreview.name: Alex
slackPreview.text: @Zero check my calendar, emails, and Linear tasks since yesterday and write me a work summary

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Work Summary: Mar 23–24

Meetings
• Daily standups (10–10:30 AM)
• VM0 User Interview w/ Daniel Miller

Engineering
• PR #5467 merged, firewall permissions
• PR #6277 in review. Lighthouse at 36

steps.title: Zero checks your calendar
steps.description: Zero reads your Google Calendar events from the past 24 hours and extracts meeting names, times, and attendees.

steps.title: Zero scans email and tasks
steps.description: Zero checks Gmail for relevant threads and Linear for tasks you updated, completed, or were assigned since yesterday.

steps.title: Formatted summary
steps.description: Zero compiles everything into a clean, copy-paste-ready summary organized by Meetings, Engineering work, and action items.

nextActions.title: Post directly to a channel
nextActions.description: Have Zero post the summary to #standup
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero post this summary to #standup and tag @team-eng.

nextActions.title: Add context
nextActions.description: Ask Zero to include blockers or priorities
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero also check my Linear for any blocked tasks and add them as blockers in the summary.

nextActions.title: Make it daily
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero every weekday at 9:45am, write my standup summary and DM it to me.

integrations.description: OAuth connection to Google Calendar. Zero needs read access to your events.
OAuth connection to Gmail. Zero needs read access to scan recent threads.
OAuth connection to Linear. Zero reads your assigned and updated tasks.

tips: Tell Zero your standup format if it differs from the default. "use the format: Yesterday / Today / Blockers".
Specify the time window. "since yesterday 5pm" if you work late.
Combine with Sentry triage for a complete engineering morning brief.

kol-cold-outreach.title: Research a KOL on X and draft a personalized cold email
kol-cold-outreach.description: Give Zero an X handle. It reads their last 30 posts, understands their style and interests, writes a personalized outreach email under 150 words, and saves it as a Gmail draft.

headings.scenario: Why generic cold outreach gets ignored
headings.prompt: How to research a KOL and draft personalized outreach
headings.steps: How Zero analyzes X profiles and crafts tailored emails
headings.nextActions: Score KOL fit, batch multiple outreach drafts, or track in Notion CRM
headings.integrations: Required integrations: X, Gmail, and Notion
headings.tips: Best practices for AI-assisted KOL outreach

kol-cold-outreach.scenario: You found a KOL who'd be perfect for a partnership, but writing a cold email that doesn't feel generic takes 20 minutes of research, reading their posts, understanding what they care about, finding the right angle. You give Zero their X handle and get a personalized draft in seconds.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero read @swyx's last 30 X posts to understand their style and interests. Write a personalized cold outreach email under 150 words about a VM0 partnership. Reference something specific they posted recently. Save it as a Gmail draft.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero draft a cold email to @swyx about VM0, based on their X posts. Save as Gmail draft.

promptVariants.label: Batch
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero for each handle in my Notion KOL list marked as "Not contacted", research their X profile and draft a personalized outreach email. Save all as Gmail drafts.

slackPreview.name: Scarlett
slackPreview.text: @Zero read @swyx's last 30 X posts, understand his style, and draft a cold outreach email for a VM0 partnership. Save as Gmail draft.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Done. Analyzed @swyx's recent content:
• Focus: AI agents, developer tools, open source
• Tone: Technical but conversational
• Audience fit: High (9/10)

Gmail draft saved: "Agents that ship real work, thought you'd find this interesting"

steps.title: Zero reads the KOL's X profile
steps.description: Zero pulls the last 30 posts, analyzes content themes, tone, audience, and engagement patterns to build a profile of what this person cares about.

steps.title: Personalized email drafted
steps.description: Zero writes a concise outreach email that references specific content the KOL posted, explains the relevance of your product, and uses a tone that matches their style.

steps.title: Draft saved to Gmail
steps.description: The email is saved as a Gmail draft ready for your review. Zero also updates the KOL's status in your Notion CRM if connected.

nextActions.title: Score KOL fit
nextActions.description: Rate how well a KOL matches your audience
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero analyze @swyx's audience overlap with VM0's ICP. Score 1-10 with reasoning and save to Notion.

nextActions.title: Batch outreach
nextActions.description: Draft emails for multiple KOLs at once
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero for the top 10 KOLs in my Notion list, research each and draft personalized outreach emails.

integrations.description: Zero reads public posts to understand the KOL's style and interests.
Zero saves the drafted email to your Gmail drafts.
Optional, track KOL status and fit scores in your Notion CRM.

tips: Give Zero context about your product angle. "focus on how VM0 helps developer tool companies".
Ask for multiple variants. "draft 2 versions: one casual, one professional".
Always review before sending. Zero gets the research right, but the final voice should be yours.

file-bugs-from-slack.title: File bugs from Slack without switching context
file-bugs-from-slack.description: Describe the issue in plain language. Zero creates a formatted GitHub issue, labels it, and assigns the right person.

headings.scenario: Why filing GitHub issues from Slack saves context switching
headings.prompt: How to create GitHub issues from a Slack message
headings.steps: How Zero parses your message and creates a formatted issue
headings.nextActions: Add details, batch file issues, or automate triage
headings.tips: Best practices for filing bugs via Slack

file-bugs-from-slack.scenario: You just noticed a UX bug during a demo. Instead of opening GitHub, finding the repo, writing a formatted issue, and assigning someone, you describe it in Slack. Zero creates the issue, adds labels, and assigns the right person. You never leave the conversation.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero create issue: pressing ESC in the schedule dialog closes it immediately even with unsaved edits. Should ask for confirmation first. Assign to Lancy. Label as bug, platform. Priority medium.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero bug: ESC closes schedule dialog without save confirmation. Assign Lancy.

promptVariants.label: Scheduled
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero every Friday at 4pm, check #bugs channel for any unresolved bug reports and create GitHub issues for each one.

slackPreview.name: Alex
slackPreview.text: @Zero create issue: pressing ESC in the schedule dialog closes it immediately even with unsaved edits. Should ask for confirmation first. Assign to Lancy.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Issue created:
#6260: bug: ESC key closes schedule dialog without confirming
Assigned to Lancy · Priority: Medium

steps.title: Zero parses the request
steps.description: Zero understands the bug description, identifies the assignee, and infers appropriate labels (bug, platform) and priority from context.

steps.title: Issue created on GitHub
steps.description: Zero creates a properly formatted GitHub issue with a clear title, description, labels, and assignment, all from your natural-language Slack message.

steps.title: Confirmation in Slack
steps.description: Zero replies in the same thread with the issue number, title, assignee, and a direct link so you can verify without leaving Slack.

nextActions.title: Add more detail
nextActions.description: Attach screenshots or steps to reproduce
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero add to #6260: steps to reproduce. 1. Open schedule dialog 2. Type something 3. Press ESC. Expected: confirmation dialog.

nextActions.title: Batch file issues
nextActions.examplePrompt:
@Zero create 3 issues from these bugs:
1. ESC dialog close (Lancy)
2. Date picker off by one day (James)
3. Avatar upload fails on Safari (Yuma)

nextActions.title: Automate triage
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero watch #bugs, when someone posts a message starting with "bug:", auto-create a GitHub issue and reply with the link.

integrations.description: OAuth connection to GitHub. Zero needs read/write access to create and manage issues.
Zero reads your message and replies in the same thread.

tips: Include the assignee name. Zero matches Slack display names to GitHub usernames.
Mention labels explicitly if you want specific ones, otherwise Zero infers from context.
Works for feature requests too, just say "feature request" instead of "bug".

slack-triage.title: Cut through Slack noise and surface what needs your attention
slack-triage.description: Zero scans your unread Slack messages, filters out bots and noise, and shows you only the messages that actually need your action today, sorted by urgency.

headings.scenario: Why Slack overload causes missed action items
headings.prompt: How to ask Zero to triage your unread Slack messages
headings.steps: How Zero filters noise and surfaces actionable messages
headings.nextActions: Reply directly, create tasks, or automate daily triage
headings.tips: Best practices for Slack message triage

slack-triage.scenario: You open Slack after a 2-hour focus block and there are 47 unread messages across 12 channels. Most are bot notifications, automated alerts, and conversations that don't need you. Somewhere in the noise are 3 things that actually need your response. Instead of scrolling through everything, you ask Zero to find what matters.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero scan my unread Slack messages from the last 12 hours. Filter out bots, automated notifications, and threads I'm not tagged in. Show me only messages that need my action today, sorted by urgency. For each one, tell me who's waiting and what they need.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero what needs my attention in Slack right now?

promptVariants.label: Scheduled
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero every morning at 9am and after lunch at 1pm, scan my unreads and DM me what needs action.

slackPreview.name: Lancy
slackPreview.text: @Zero scan my unread Slack from the last 12 hours. Filter out bots and noise. Show me only what needs my action today.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Found 47 unread messages. 5 need your action:

1. @Ethan asked for your design review on PR #6312
2. @James needs approval on the Stripe migration plan
3. #support has a P0 from a customer mentioning churn
4. @Scarlett tagged you in a KOL outreach decision
5. Sprint review moved to 3pm (was 2pm)

steps.title: Zero scans your channels
steps.description: Zero reads your unread messages across all channels, filtering out bot messages, automated CI/CD alerts, and threads where you're not mentioned or needed.

steps.title: Messages classified by urgency
steps.description: Each remaining message is assessed: is someone waiting on you? Is there a deadline? Is it a decision that's blocking others? Zero ranks them by how urgently they need your response.

steps.title: Actionable summary delivered
steps.description: Zero DMs you a numbered list of what needs attention, with who's asking, what they need, and a direct link to each thread. Everything else is safely ignored.

nextActions.title: Reply through Zero
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero reply to #1: "Looks good, approved. Ship it." And for #3, create a P0 Linear issue and assign to James.

nextActions.title: Make it a routine
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero every weekday at 9am, scan my unreads from overnight and DM me the action items.

integrations.description: Zero reads your unread messages and DMs you the triage summary.
Optional: create tasks directly from triaged messages.
Optional: Zero checks your calendar to flag meeting-related messages.

tips: Tell Zero which channels matter most to you so it can prioritize accordingly.
Ask Zero to learn your noise patterns over time: "always skip messages from @github-bot and @vercel-bot".
Combine with the standup use case: triage first, then generate your standup from the action items.

employee-onboarding.title: Onboard new teammates with one Slack message
employee-onboarding.description: Tell Zero who's joining and when. It creates their Notion onboarding page, schedules welcome meetings on Google Calendar, posts a Slack intro, and DMs them their first-week agenda.

headings.scenario: Why manual onboarding checklists always miss something
headings.prompt: How to automate new hire onboarding with Zero
headings.steps: How Zero sets up Notion, Calendar, and Slack for a new teammate
headings.nextActions: Add access provisioning or customize by role
headings.integrations: Required integrations: Slack, Notion, Google Calendar, and Gmail
headings.tips: Best practices for automated employee onboarding

employee-onboarding.scenario: A new teammate starts next Monday. Someone needs to create their onboarding doc, schedule intro meetings with 5 people, post a welcome message, and send them a first-week agenda. It's 45 minutes of coordination that happens the same way every time. You tell Zero once and it handles everything.

promptVariants.label: Detailed
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero onboard Sarah Chen joining as Product Designer on April 14. Duplicate the Notion onboarding template, schedule week-1 intro meetings with Ethan, Lancy, James, Yuma, and Scarlett on Google Calendar. Schedule a 30-day check-in. Post a welcome to #general and DM Sarah her first-week agenda.

promptVariants.label: Quick
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero onboard Sarah Chen, Product Designer, starting April 14. Full setup.

promptVariants.label: Template
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero when someone posts in #new-hires with format "Name / Role / Start Date", auto-run the full onboarding workflow.

slackPreview.name: Lancy
slackPreview.text: @Zero onboard Sarah Chen, joining as Product Designer on April 14. Set up everything.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Onboarding for Sarah Chen is ready:

✔ Notion page created from template
✔ Week-1 intro meetings scheduled
✔ 30-day check-in on May 14
✔ Welcome posted to #general
✔ DM sent with first-week agenda

steps.title: Notion onboarding page created
steps.description: Zero duplicates your onboarding template in Notion, fills in the new hire's name, role, start date, and manager. Links to relevant docs and team resources.

steps.title: Meetings scheduled
steps.description: Zero creates intro meetings with specified teammates on Google Calendar during the first week, plus a 30-day check-in. All calendar invites include context about what to discuss.

steps.title: Slack welcome and DM sent
steps.description: Zero posts a welcome message to #general introducing the new hire, then DMs them directly with their first-week agenda, links to their Notion page, and key contacts.

nextActions.title: Customize by role
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero for engineers, also add a pairing session with the tech lead and link to the architecture docs in the onboarding page.

nextActions.title: Automate triggers
nextActions.description: Run onboarding from a channel post
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero whenever someone posts in #new-hires with the format "Name / Role / Start Date", run the full onboarding automatically.

integrations.description: Zero posts the welcome message and DMs the new hire.
Zero creates the onboarding page from your template.
Zero schedules intro meetings and check-ins.
Optional, send a welcome email with logistics and links.

tips: Create a Notion onboarding template first. Zero duplicates it and fills in the details.
List the people who should have intro meetings. Zero handles the calendar coordination.
Works for contractors and interns too, just adjust the template and meeting list.

build-with-v0.title: Turn a Slack idea into an interactive prototype instantly
build-with-v0.description: When an idea sparks mid-conversation, describe it to Zero. It uses v0 to generate a clickable React prototype and posts the live preview link back in the thread — before the discussion moves on.

headings.scenario: Why ideas get lost before anyone can see them
headings.prompt: How to turn a Slack idea into a prototype with Zero
headings.steps: How Zero goes from your description to a live, clickable UI
headings.nextActions: Refine, share, or hand off to engineering
headings.tips: Best practices for rapid idea prototyping with v0

build-with-v0.scenario: You're in a Slack thread debating how a feature should work. Someone proposes a new UI pattern — a command palette, a settings panel, a multi-step wizard. Words aren't landing. Sketching on a whiteboard isn't an option. Scheduling a Figma session pushes the decision to next week. You describe the idea to Zero in plain language. It calls v0, generates a fully interactive React prototype, and posts the live preview link right back in the thread — in under a minute. Everyone can click through it before the conversation moves on.

promptVariants.label: New UI idea
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero prototype this: a command palette that lets users search agents, recent runs, and connectors. Keyboard-navigable, fuzzy search, shows a preview panel on the right side.

promptVariants.label: From thread context
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero based on the flow we just described above, build a quick v0 prototype so the team can see it before we decide.

promptVariants.label: Iterate
promptVariants.prompt: @Zero update the prototype — add a filter tab at the top for 'Agents / Runs / Connectors' and highlight the active item in orange. Regenerate.

slackPreview.name: Ethan
slackPreview.text: @Zero prototype this: a command palette that lets users search agents, recent runs, and connectors. Keyboard-navigable, fuzzy search, shows a preview on the right.

slackPreview.name: Zero
slackPreview.text:
Here's the interactive prototype:
v0.dev/r/cmd-palette-preview

Includes fuzzy search across agents, runs, and connectors. Arrow-key navigation. Right-side preview panel updates on hover. Click to open.

steps.title: Describe the idea in plain language
steps.description: No spec, no wireframe required. Tell Zero what the UI should do — the components, the interactions, the data it surfaces. Paste a rough sketch description or just riff from the thread.

steps.title: Zero generates the prototype via v0
steps.description: Zero translates your description into a structured v0 prompt and calls the v0 API. v0 produces a fully interactive React component — real buttons, real states, real navigation.

steps.title: Live preview link posted in Slack
steps.description: Zero posts the v0 preview URL back in the same thread. Teammates can click through the prototype immediately, on any device, without installing anything or checking out code.

nextActions.title: Refine in the thread
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero update the prototype — the search input should have an icon on the left, and empty state should show recent items instead of nothing.

nextActions.title: Share for async feedback
nextActions.description: Post to a broader channel or DM stakeholders
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero share the v0 prototype link to #product with the message: 'Quick prototype of the command palette idea — feedback welcome before Thursday.'

nextActions.title: Hand off to engineering
nextActions.description: Export the generated code into the repo
nextActions.examplePrompt: @Zero take the v0 prototype code and open a PR in vm0-ai/vm0 under turbo/apps/platform/src/components/CommandPalette.tsx for the team to build on.

integrations.description: Zero sends your idea as a generation prompt to v0 and retrieves the interactive prototype URL. Connect your v0 account to enable this.
Zero reads your idea from the Slack thread and posts the prototype link back in the same conversation.

tips: Describe behavior, not just appearance. 'Clicking a row expands details below it' gets a more accurate prototype than 'expandable rows'.
Reference existing UI patterns by name — 'like a VS Code command palette' or 'like Notion's slash menu' — to anchor v0's generation.
Use the iterate prompt immediately after the first result. One round of refinement usually gets you 90% of the way there.

hero.title: Zero, your trustworthy AI teammate for real work.
hero.subtitle: Zero connects to 100+ tools and does the work. Reports, triage, outreach, research. In Slack or on the web.
hero.seedBanner: Check out our $14M seed round fundraising blog.

worksForYou.heading: What Zero actually does for you

teammateCard.title: A teammate that reads, thinks, and delivers.
teammateCard.description: “Summarize my week from Calendar, Linear, and Slack.” Zero pulls context from your tools, connects the dots, and hands you a finished report. Like briefing a colleague, except it takes seconds.

slackCard.title: Ask in Slack, get answers from all your tools.
slackCard.description: @Zero in any channel. It pulls from your calendar, email, Linear, GitHub — and replies with a summary your team can actually use. No tab-switching, no dashboards.

syncedToolsCard.title: From discovery to outreach, agents do the legwork.
syncedToolsCard.description: Tell Zero who you’re looking for. Its agents crawl social platforms, build prospect lists, and draft personalized outreach so your team focuses on closing, not searching.

connectors.heading: Works with 100+ tools you already use
connectors.description: Slack, GitHub, Gmail, Notion, Linear, Sentry, and more. Zero connects to your stack so it can act, not just answer.

security.heading: Your data stays yours
security.permissionTitle: You control what Zero can access
security.permissionDesc: Set read and write permissions per tool, per agent. Zero only touches what you explicitly allow.
security.isolatedDescPart1: Every task runs in its own microVM. Credentials never leave the sandbox. Fully auditable. And

intelligence.heading: Gets smarter the more you use it
intelligence.memoryDesc: Past decisions, project context, your preferences — Zero carries it all forward. No re-explaining, no lost context between sessions.
intelligence.scheduleDesc: Zero runs autonomous recurring tasks, daily error scans, tech debt reports, morning briefs, without being prompted.
intelligence.subAgentsDesc: Create agents for specific jobs. One for research, one for triage, one for outreach. They work in parallel so you don't.
intelligence.toolOrchDesc: Zero selects and chains the right tools from 100+ available integrations. You describe the goal; Zero figures out the steps.
intelligence.identityDesc: "My PRs," "assign to me." Zero resolves your identity across GitHub, Slack, and Linear automatically. No setup required.

cta.title: You decide what matters. Zero handles the rest.
cta.subtitle: Start free. Use it in Slack or on the web. No credit card required.

securityPage.title: Security
securityPage.intro: When you hand work to an AI agent, your biggest concerns are data safety, privacy, and staying in control. VM0 was designed from day one with all of this in mind.

isolatedExecution.title: Isolated execution
isolatedExecution.description: Every agent run happens inside a completely isolated private environment. When the run finishes, the environment is destroyed automatically, nothing is left behind. Think of it like a disposable glove: clean, safe, and reliable.
isolatedExecution.detail: Powered by Firecracker microVMs with hardware-level KVM isolation, not containers. Each run gets its own network namespace from a pre-allocated pool of 16,000+.

secretsNeverExposed.title: Secrets never exposed
secretsNeverExposed.description: Connecting Gmail, Slack, or GitHub? Your tokens and credentials are securely managed for you. The agent can use them, but it can never see or extract them. Even if something goes wrong in the agent's code, your accounts stay safe.
secretsNeverExposed.detail: Credentials are injected at the network layer via transparent MITM proxy. Agent code never touches raw tokens. Outbound requests are scanned to prevent secret leakage.

startsInSeconds.title: Starts in seconds
startsInSeconds.description: We've done extensive optimization under the hood so your agent goes from trigger to running in tens of milliseconds. Fast, because we put in the hard work at the infrastructure level. You just experience the result.
startsInSeconds.detail: Overlayfs with shared read-only rootfs for zero-copy boot. VM memory snapshots pre-warm the entire runtime stack, restore instead of cold start.

fullAuditTrail.title: Full audit trail
fullAuditTrail.description: Every action the agent takes, which services it accessed, which APIs it called, what it produced, is logged. If something goes wrong, there's a clear record. If nothing goes wrong, you still have peace of mind.
fullAuditTrail.detail: Complete HTTP/HTTPS traffic logged per run (JSONL). Immutable, content-addressed artifacts stored on Cloudflare R2 with SHA-256 integrity.

openSource.title: Open source
openSource.description: The core platform is open source. You can inspect the code, audit the security model, and contribute. Transparent by default.
openSource.detail: Core platform on GitHub. Regular third-party penetration testing. SOC 2 Type II compliance in progress.
